title: LAN and WAN in context
objective: Kick off the week's topic and build the right vocabulary.
week_focus: Networking foundations: models, media, devices, and topologies
checkpoint: Can describe how data moves through a simple network.
resource_names: Cisco CCST Networking / Network Technician path
plain_language_intro: Today covers LAN and WAN within networking foundations: models, media, devices, and topologies.
mini_lecture_markdown:
The week focus is Networking foundations: models, media, devices, and topologies, and today's objective is kick off the week's topic and build the right vocabulary. The main terms in scope are LAN and WAN. The focus is on what each one does, how it operates, and how it affects the rest of the system.

LAN is local area network that connects devices across a limited physical space such as a room, office, or campus. It provides high-speed local communication among nearby systems and shared resources. Mechanically, it uses local switching and addressing so devices exchange frames directly across a contained network segment. WAN is wide area network that connects distant sites across provider infrastructure or large geographic areas. It carries traffic between remote networks that cannot be joined by one local switching domain. Mechanically, it uses routed links across carrier or internet paths so separate local networks can exchange traffic over distance. Together, these terms describe where work happens, how state changes, and what the visible outcome looks like when the system is operating normally.

Learn what networks do, why layers exist, and how LAN, WAN, and internet paths differ. The goal is to connect the concept to actual behavior, output, or system state.

Resources such as Cisco CCST Networking / Network Technician path and Professor Messer Network+ (N10-009) provide concrete examples for this topic. In this week, Networking foundations: models, media, devices, and topologies is easier to understand when the examples are tied to visible behavior and concrete outputs.

Most scenario questions on LAN and WAN hinge on clues such as output, access, performance, routing, logging, or startup behavior.

Another useful angle is to note where the concept sits in the larger system, what it depends on, and what would change if it failed or were configured incorrectly.

The practical value of the topic is usually clearest when you follow one path from input to processing to output and identify the point where the concept changes the result.
exam_relevance_markdown: This material is most likely to appear as a short scenario where you must identify how LAN and WAN affect the outcome. That can mean choosing the best answer for a symptom, distinguishing two similar terms, following a process step in the right order, or recognizing why one configuration works while another fails. The exam signal is applied reasoning: knowing what the concept does in context and what evidence points to it.
key_distinctions: LAN vs WAN — LAN: Local area network that connects devices across a limited physical space such as a room, office, or campus. WAN: Wide area network that connects distant sites across provider infrastructure or large geographic areas.

WAN vs LAN — WAN: Wide area network that connects distant sites across provider infrastructure or large geographic areas. LAN: Local area network that connects devices across a limited physical space such as a room, office, or campus.
Purpose vs mechanism — be able to separate what LAN is for from how it actually performs that job.
Recognition vs diagnosis — knowing the term name is not enough; know what evidence would make you pick it in a scenario.
mental_model_markdown: Use a pipeline model for this day: input arrives, a component or service processes it, state changes occur, and an output or symptom becomes visible to the user, administrator, or analyst. LAN: uses local switching and addressing so devices exchange frames directly across a contained network segment WAN: uses routed links across carrier or internet paths so separate local networks can exchange traffic over distance That sequence is the main mental model for the topic.
self_check_prompts: Explain the difference between LAN and WAN without using the words "better" or "faster" as your whole answer.
Describe what LAN does, what input it works on, and what evidence would show that it is functioning correctly.
Walk through today's objective in sequence and say which step would fail first if the system were misconfigured.
Connect today's glossary terms to this week's deliverable: what part of the artifact proves that you understand the mechanism and not just the label?
Give one realistic troubleshooting or security scenario where today's concepts would change the next decision you make.
quick_recap: Today covered LAN and WAN in terms of role, mechanism, and system effect.
